D1.1: pipeline generation — increasing the volume of qualified leads entering the sales funnel
D1.2: conversion optimization — improving the rate at which existing leads become paying customers
D1.3: revenue deepening — growing wallet share from the existing customer base through upselling
D1.4: portfolio expansion — introducing new offerings to existing customers through cross-selling
D1.5: customer retention — strengthening satisfaction and loyalty to reduce churn and increase lifetime value
D1.6: speed of delivery — reducing turnaround time to serve more customers with the same capacity
D1.7: quality and compliance — elevating product standards and regulatory adherence to protect market access
D1.8: margin improvement — reducing operational waste to increase profitability without growing revenue

D2.1: Both the current state and target are precise — the company tracks this KPI rigorously and has a data-backed target
D2.2: The current state is measured, and the target is a stretch goal based on the founder's growth ambition
D2.3: The current state is measured, and the target is defined by what the market demands to remain competitive
D2.4: The gap between today and the target is felt but not tracked rigorously — the target is clear, the baseline is approximate
D2.5: Both current and target are approximate — the company has a range, not a number
D2.6: The current state is known, but the target depends on what turns out to be realistic once execution begins
D2.7: The target is defined from peer benchmarks — the current state is where the company falls short of the industry
D2.8: This is the first time putting a number on this — the gap was always felt but never quantified

D3.1: Revenue crosses the next major milestone — ₹50Cr, ₹100Cr, or ₹200Cr — a threshold that changes the company's competitive position
D3.2: The growth model starts compounding — not just linear revenue addition but multiplicative gains across the business
D3.3: A new market or geography becomes viable — the KPI improvement unlocks expansion that was previously out of reach
D3.4: The business becomes investable or exit-ready — the improvement makes the company attractive for institutional capital or acquisition
D3.5: Profitability step-changes — growth starts paying for itself instead of consuming cash
D3.6: The team scales — a leadership layer emerges below the founder, creating organizational capacity
D3.7: Competitive position locks in — the window of opportunity closes in the company's favor
D3.8: The business becomes what the founder originally envisioned when they started the company

D7.1: The founder focuses on vision and strategy — no longer chasing daily execution across every function
D7.2: The business runs without the founder in every room — operational independence from the founder's constant presence
D7.3: The founder's best people grow into the leaders they need — the next generation of management emerges
D7.4: The founder proves the company can compound — not just grow year to year but build durable momentum
D7.5: The founder creates something worth more than their time in it — the company's value transcends the founder's operational involvement
D7.6: The founder builds the leadership layer they've been wanting — delegation becomes real, not theoretical
D7.7: The founder builds what they set out to build when they started the company — the original vision comes to life
D7.8: The founder enjoys running this business again — it becomes energizing, not draining

I3.1: A dedicated person with no other responsibilities — singular focus on driving this KPI
I3.2: A senior leader is carving out time for it — capable but splitting attention with other priorities
I3.3: The founder will drive it personally — with a system supporting them rather than carrying the load alone
I3.4: A small team focused only on improvement — a dedicated unit that doesn't get pulled into operations
I3.5: Each department head owns their piece — distributed ownership with coordination needed
I3.6: Someone from outside brings structure — external capability to design what the organization can't build alone
I3.7: The right person doesn't exist in the organization yet — the role needs to be created or hired for
I3.8: The founder knows who should drive it, but that person is buried in day-to-day operations — their time is consumed by routine delivery, leaving no bandwidth for systematic improvement

I9.1: Previous attempts needed a dedicated person — not someone splitting time with routine operations
I9.2: Previous attempts lacked a method — there was intent and energy but no structured, repeatable approach
I9.3: Previous attempts ran too long without checkpoints — by the time failure was visible, months of effort were wasted
I9.4: Previous attempts needed external structure — the organization couldn't build the framework on its own
I9.5: Previous attempts lacked the founder's involvement in reviews — delegation without oversight led to drift
I9.6: Previous attempts started too large — the next attempt should prove the concept small before expanding
I9.7: Previous attempts tracked outcomes but not activities — the work wasn't visible until results appeared
I9.8: This is the first real attempt — no prior failure to learn from

I12.1: The process works, it just needs technology to go faster — the logic stays the same, manual steps get automated
I12.2: The process works but manual bottlenecks slow it down — technology replaces specific manual steps without changing process design
I12.3: The process exists but isn't delivering results — specific steps need diagnosis and redesign
I12.4: The process was designed for a different scale — what worked at ₹50Cr is breaking at ₹200Cr
I12.5: Parts of the process exist, other parts are missing — the gaps need to be built, not the whole system
I12.6: No designed process exists for this function — it's ad-hoc, relationship-driven, or founder-dependent
I12.7: The process has been improved before and hit a ceiling — a fundamentally different approach is needed
I12.8: This function doesn't exist in the company — it's being built from scratch

F2.1: ₹500 Crore+ annual revenue — large mid-market enterprise
F2.2: ₹200–500 Crore annual revenue — upper mid-market, established operations
F2.4: ₹50–100 Crore annual revenue — proven business model with room to grow
F2.5: ₹25–50 Crore annual revenue — emerging business with growth potential
F2.6: ₹10–25 Crore annual revenue — early stage, founder-driven
F2.7: Below ₹10 Crore annual revenue — early-stage venture

C7.1: ERP fully implemented and integrated across departments — real-time dashboards, structured costing, data-driven decisions
C7.2: ERP implemented in core functions — structured costing exists, some manual gaps remain
C7.3: ERP implemented but partially adopted — some functions on the system, others still on Excel or manual processes
C7.4: No ERP, but structured processes exist — formal SOPs, costing models, and disciplined tracking through spreadsheets
C7.5: Digital tools adopted piecemeal — accounting software, spreadsheets for planning, messaging apps for coordination, but no integration
C7.6: Systems investment planned or in progress — the company recognizes the need and has begun implementation
C7.7: Operations run on the founder's memory and informal coordination — no structured tracking or documentation
C7.8: Actively resistant to systems — leadership does not see value in structured operational tools

K1.1: Direct relationship established — the decision-maker is engaged and actively participating in conversations
K1.2: Decision-maker identified and contacted — responsive, scheduling or in early conversation
K1.3: Decision-maker fully profiled from research — name, title, and background known, not yet contacted
K1.4: Decision-maker identified by name and role — basic profile known, background unclear
K1.5: Decision-maker role known but specific person uncertain — multiple candidates
K1.6: Decision-maker contacted through an intermediary — not reached directly
K1.7: Decision-maker identified but access is gated — an assistant or family member screens all contact
K1.8: Decision-maker not identified — company structure unclear
